{
  "schema_version": "stella.literature_hvs_candidates.v0.1",
  "generated_at": "2026-06-13T03:54:00",
  "paper": {
    "arxiv_id": "2003.12766",
    "bibcode": "2020A&A...637A..53K",
    "title": "Hypervelocity Stars in the Gaia era. Revisiting the most extreme stars from the MMT survey",
    "month": "2020-03",
    "source_note_json": "notes/2020/2020-03/2020-03.json",
    "links": {
      "abs": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.12766",
      "pdf": "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2003.12766"
    }
  },
  "inputs": {
    "paper_dir": "literature/2003.12766",
    "audit_path": "literature/2003.12766/audit.json",
    "catalog_review_path": "literature/2003.12766/catalog_review.json",
    "catalog_extraction_path": "literature/2003.12766/catalog_extraction.json",
    "ecsv_paths": [
      "literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-atmos-results.ecsv",
      "literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-B1085.ecsv",
      "literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-hvs5.ecsv",
      "literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-stellar_params.ecsv",
      "literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-kinematic-AS.ecsv",
      "literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-kinematic-TF.ecsv"
    ]
  },
  "extraction": {
    "status": "candidates_found",
    "extracted_at": "2026-06-13T03:54:00",
    "extractor": "agent",
    "summary": "The paper reanalyzes 40 stars from the MMT HVS survey using new spectroscopic models and Gaia DR2 astrometry. 37 stars have full 6D kinematics and are presented as hypervelocity star candidates with bound/unbound probabilities. The extraction includes all 37 kinematic sample stars as candidates possibly unbound from the Milky Way.",
    "tooling": {
      "agent_runtime": "",
      "model_id": "",
      "prompt_version": "",
      "request_parameters": {}
    }
  },
  "method_chain": [
    {
      "id": "step-01",
      "depends_on": [],
      "step_type": "input_catalog",
      "summary": "MMT HVS survey sample of 42 B-type stars with MMT spectra, selected from Brown et al. (2014). Spectra are flux-calibrated for analysis.",
      "inputs": [],
      "outputs": ["MMT spectra", "object list"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 99,
          "end_line": 99,
          "context": "In this work, which we regard as Paper~III in this series, we extend the spectrophotometric analysis to 40 out of the 42 high-velocity outliers of the MMT HVS sample."
        },
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 144,
          "end_line": 144,
          "context": "The spectra analyzed in this work were taken during the MMT HVS survey and kindly provided by Warren Brown."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-02",
      "depends_on": [],
      "step_type": "input_catalog",
      "summary": "Photometric data compiled from GALEX, Gaia DR2, SDSS DR12, Pan-STARRS1, SkyMapper DR1.1, BATC, VST-ATLAS-DR3, VST-KiDS-DR3, APASS-DR9, UKIDSS-DR9, VISTA, used to construct spectral energy distributions (SEDs).",
      "inputs": [],
      "outputs": ["multi-band photometry"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 272,
          "end_line": 272,
          "context": "Photometric data were compiled from a variety of surveys covering the ultraviolet, the optical, and the infrared mainly using the VizieR catalog access tool."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-03",
      "depends_on": [],
      "step_type": "astrometric_calibration",
      "summary": "Proper motions obtained from Gaia DR2 and HST (WFC3/ACS). For a subsample of 15 stars HST proper motions are available. The more precise measurement is adopted for each star; HST data used for HVS1, HVS10, HVS12, HVS13; Gaia DR2 for B711; for the remaining stars Gaia DR2 is used. RUWE < 1.4 for all stars.",
      "inputs": ["Gaia DR2 proper motions", "HST proper motions"],
      "outputs": ["adopted proper motions"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 288,
          "end_line": 288,
          "context": "Proper motions were mainly taken from Gaia DR2. For a subsample of 15 stars, pre-Gaia proper motions measured with the HST instruments WFC3 and ACS were available. ... In analogy to Paper II, we chose the more precise measurement when Gaia as well as HST proper motions were available, which implies that we used HST data for HVS1, HVS10, HVS12, and HVS13 and Gaia DR2 data for B711."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-04",
      "depends_on": ["step-01", "step-03"],
      "step_type": "sample_selection",
      "summary": "From the 42 MMT HVS sample, 40 stars have available spectra; 37 have complete astrometric data (proper motions). The kinematic analysis is performed on these 37 stars. Stars HVS11, HVS14, HVS23 lack proper motions and are excluded from kinematics.",
      "inputs": ["survey sample", "proper motions"],
      "outputs": ["kinematic sample of 37 stars"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 99,
          "end_line": 99,
          "context": "In this work ... we extend the spectrophotometric analysis to 40 out of the 42 high-velocity outliers of the MMT HVS sample. ... Complete astrometric data is available for 37 of them, allowing us to carry out a subsequent kinematic analysis as well."
        },
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 284,
          "end_line": 284,
          "context": "Unfortunately, proper motion measurements were not available for HVS11, HVS14, and HVS23, which is why the kinematic analysis encompasses only 37 out of the 40 stars in the sample."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-05",
      "depends_on": ["step-01"],
      "step_type": "radial_velocity_measurement",
      "summary": "Radial velocities are derived from the spectral fitting of flux-calibrated MMT spectra using a χ² minimization with model spectra. The fits simultaneously determine Teff, log g, v sin i, and radial velocity, plus reddening and distance scaling.",
      "inputs": ["MMT flux-calibrated spectra"],
      "outputs": ["radial velocities"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 150,
          "end_line": 150,
          "context": "The underlying idea was to simultaneously fit all individual spectra of a star ... with four fitting parameters ... including the radial velocity."
        },
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 110,
          "end_line": 128,
          "context": "Table with spectroscopic results including radial velocities."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-06",
      "depends_on": ["step-01"],
      "step_type": "stellar_parameter_inference",
      "summary": "Effective temperature, surface gravity, and projected rotational velocity are determined from the same spectral fits as radial velocity. The ADS grid of synthetic spectra (solar composition, T_eff 9000–16000 K, log g 3.0–4.8) is used. Results are listed in Table tab:atmos-results.",
      "inputs": ["MMT spectra", "model grid"],
      "outputs": ["T_eff", "log g", "v sin i"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 150,
          "end_line": 150,
          "context": "The underlying idea was to simultaneously fit all individual spectra ... with four fitting parameters for the stellar spectrum: the effective temperature T_eff, the surface gravity log(g), the projected rotational velocity v sin i, and the radial velocity v_rad."
        },
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 110,
          "end_line": 128,
          "context": "Table tab:atmos-results summarizing spectroscopic parameters."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-07",
      "depends_on": ["step-02", "step-06"],
      "step_type": "distance_estimation",
      "summary": "Spectrophotometric distances are derived by constructing observed SEDs from the compiled photometry, fitting synthetic SEDs (Atlas12 models with fixed T_eff and log g from spectroscopy) to obtain angular diameter and color excess E(B-V). The distance is computed as d = 2 R / Θ, where stellar radius R is derived from the spectroscopic surface gravity and mass from evolutionary tracks.",
      "inputs": ["multi-band photometry", "T_eff", "log g", "evolutionary tracks"],
      "outputs": ["spectrophotometric distances"],
      "parameters": [
        {
          "name": "other",
          "raw_value": "R_V = 3.1",
          "value": "3.1",
          "source_refs": [
            {
              "kind": "text",
              "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
              "start_line": 276,
              "end_line": 276,
              "context": "We used the extinction curve by Fitzpatrick (1999) with R_V fixed at 3.1."
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 276,
          "end_line": 280,
          "context": "The resulting observed SEDs were compared to synthetic ones ... The angular diameters Θ ... were combined with the stellar radii R = (G M / g)^{1/2} ... to calculate the spectrophotometric distances d."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-08",
      "depends_on": ["step-06"],
      "step_type": "stellar_parameter_inference",
      "summary": "Stellar masses, ages, radii, luminosities, and critical rotation ratios are derived by comparing the (T_eff, log g) position to evolutionary tracks for rotating MS stars (Georgy et al. 2013). Stellar radii used for distance estimation.",
      "inputs": ["T_eff", "log g", "evolutionary tracks"],
      "outputs": ["mass", "age", "radius", "luminosity"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 226,
          "end_line": 226,
          "context": "Under this assumption, stellar masses M, ages τ, radii R, luminosities L, and ratios of actual angular velocity to critical velocity Ω/Ω_crit can be derived by comparing the stars' position in the Kiel diagram with theoretical predictions."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-09",
      "depends_on": [],
      "step_type": "solar_position_and_motion",
      "summary": "Assumptions for conversion to Galactocentric coordinates: Sun located on negative x-axis, R0 = 8.3 kpc, solar peculiar motion and circular velocity adopted from Irrgang et al. (2018). Only R0 is explicitly stated in this paper (figure caption); other parameters are not reported herein.",
      "inputs": [],
      "outputs": ["solar Galactocentric coordinates and velocity"],
      "parameters": [
        {
          "name": "R0",
          "raw_value": "8.3 \\,kpc",
          "value": "8.3",
          "unit": "kpc",
          "source_refs": [
            {
              "kind": "text",
              "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
              "start_line": 325,
              "end_line": 325,
              "context": "three black circles with different radii are overplotted for reference: a central circle (solid; 1 kpc), the solar radius (dashed; 8.3 kpc), and the Galactic disk (dashed; 20 kpc)."
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "name": "z0",
          "raw_value": "not reported",
          "value": "",
          "source_refs": []
        },
        {
          "name": "v_circ_sun",
          "raw_value": "not reported",
          "value": "",
          "source_refs": []
        },
        {
          "name": "solar_motion_u",
          "raw_value": "not reported",
          "value": "",
          "source_refs": []
        },
        {
          "name": "solar_motion_v",
          "raw_value": "not reported",
          "value": "",
          "source_refs": []
        },
        {
          "name": "solar_motion_w",
          "raw_value": "not reported",
          "value": "",
          "source_refs": []
        }
      ],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 307,
          "end_line": 307,
          "context": "The quantities shown there are based on a right-handed Cartesian Galactic coordinate system in which the Sun is located on the negative x-axis and the z-axis points to the Galactic north pole."
        },
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 284,
          "end_line": 284,
          "context": "The strategy of the kinematic analysis is identical to that of Paper II."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-10",
      "depends_on": ["step-05", "step-03", "step-07", "step-09"],
      "step_type": "velocity_calculation",
      "summary": "Full 6D phase space is constructed from positions, proper motions, radial velocities, and spectrophotometric distances. Galactocentric Cartesian positions and velocities are computed using the solar position and motion assumptions. Galactic rest-frame velocity v_Grf is the magnitude of the Galactocentric velocity vector.",
      "inputs": ["RA, Dec", "proper motions", "radial velocities", "distances", "solar parameters"],
      "outputs": ["Galactocentric x,y,z, vx,vy,vz, v_Grf"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 284,
          "end_line": 284,
          "context": "Complementing our revised radial velocities and spectrophotometric distances with positions and proper motions ... gave us the full six-dimensional phase space information that is needed to calculate the trajectories."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-11",
      "depends_on": [],
      "step_type": "galactic_potential_model",
      "summary": "Two Milky Way mass models are used: Model I (revision of Allen & Santillan 1991) and Model II (based on flat rotation curve of Wilkinson & Evans 1999). Both are calibrated with Gaia DR2 data. The potential is used to compute escape velocities and for orbit integration.",
      "inputs": [],
      "outputs": ["potential models"],
      "parameters": [
        {
          "name": "potential_name",
          "raw_value": "Model I (Allen & Santillan revision)",
          "value": "Model I (Allen & Santillan revision)",
          "source_refs": [
            {
              "kind": "text",
              "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
              "start_line": 293,
              "end_line": 293,
              "context": "Model~I is a revision of the popular model by Allen & Santillan (1991)"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "name": "potential_name",
          "raw_value": "Model II (flat rotation curve)",
          "value": "Model II (flat rotation curve)",
          "source_refs": [
            {
              "kind": "text",
              "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
              "start_line": 293,
              "end_line": 293,
              "context": "Model~II is based on the flat rotation curve model by Wilkinson & Evans (1999)"
            }
          ]
        }
      ],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 291,
          "end_line": 293,
          "context": "The trajectories of all targets were computed in two different Milky Way mass models ... Model~I ... Model~II ..."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-12",
      "depends_on": ["step-10", "step-11"],
      "step_type": "orbit_integration",
      "summary": "Trajectories are traced backwards in time for up to 15 Gyr using a Monte Carlo approach with 1.5 million realizations, accounting for uncertainties in distance, radial velocity, and proper motions (including Gaia proper motion correlations). Plane-crossing events within the upper 99% confidence limit on stellar age are recorded.",
      "inputs": ["6D phase space", "potential models", "stellar ages"],
      "outputs": ["Monte Carlo orbit ensembles", "plane-crossing positions and velocities"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 284,
          "end_line": 284,
          "context": "Propagation of uncertainties ... was achieved using a standard Monte Carlo approach with 1.5 million realizations."
        },
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 297,
          "end_line": 297,
          "context": "Tracing back the trajectories of the targets also gave us information about their spatial origin ... we considered only those out of the 1.5 million Monte Carlo orbits that cross the Galactic plane within the upper 99% confidence limit for the respective stellar age."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-13",
      "depends_on": ["step-12"],
      "step_type": "escape_or_bound_assessment",
      "summary": "For each star, the local Galactic escape velocity v_esc is computed, and the Galactic rest-frame velocity v_Grf is compared to it. The bound probability P_b (in %) is derived from the fraction of Monte Carlo realizations that are bound. The quantity v_Grf - v_esc and P_b are listed in the kinematic tables.",
      "inputs": ["orbit ensembles", "potential model"],
      "outputs": ["v_esc", "v_Grf - v_esc", "P_b"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 307,
          "end_line": 307,
          "context": "The Galactic rest-frame velocity v_Grf, the local Galactic escape velocity v_esc ... are listed."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "step-14",
      "depends_on": ["step-12", "step-08"],
      "step_type": "origin_assessment",
      "summary": "Stars are classified into three categories based on the spatial origin of their orbits: unconstrained origin (disk plane-crossing contours encompass entire disk), possible outer rim origin (majority of trajectories intersect beyond 20 kpc), and disk origin (origin likely within the Galactic disk). Ejection velocities at plane crossing are derived.",
      "inputs": ["Monte Carlo plane-crossing locations", "stellar ages"],
      "outputs": ["origin categories", "ejection velocities"],
      "parameters": [],
      "source_refs": [
        {
          "kind": "text",
          "path": "literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex",
          "start_line": 329,
          "end_line": 331,
          "context": "In the following, we group the stars in three categories based on their inferred spatial origin. The first consists of stars for which the available data are insufficient to constrain the place of origin ... The second category comprises objects whose origin lies far outside the solar circle ... Finally, the third group consists of the best constrained objects."
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
